macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishna: Deaf, Mentally Impaired, and Minors Performing Shechita

micro_segments.title: Opening question: Does shechita require only physical action or does it require intention (kavana)?
The fundamental status of cheresh, shoteh, and katan as non-agents (lo bnei daat) and their capacity to effect halakhic change
Distinction between daat (comprehensive legal agency) and kavana (specific intention) in halakhic analysis

macro_segments.title: The Three Categories of Action and Intention for Minors

micro_segments.title: Category 1: Naked intention alone (machshava grudit) without action—not recognized by halakha
Category 2: Self-evident action whose purpose is clear from context (maaseh atzmo mevuat)
Category 3: The middle case—intention evident through action but with ambiguity (machshavto nikeret mitoch maasav)
Tosafot's interpretation: Halakha does not acknowledge a minor's stated intention but can recognize the type of action performed objectively
Rashi's interpretation: A minor's intention is believed only when backed by both statement and corresponding action

macro_segments.title: Case Study: Moving Fruit to the Roof (Machshavto Nikeret Mitoch Maasav)

micro_segments.title: The case of fruit brought to a roof to protect from infestation, then dew falls on it (kiyutan status)
For an adult, pure intention (even without action) gives the dew halakhic significance for tumah
For cheresh, shoteh, and katan, intention alone (even if stated) does not render fruit susceptible to tumah
Rabbi Yochanan's qualification: The middle case applies when the action itself is not completely self-evident (e.g., flipping fruit after dew)

macro_segments.title: Resolution: Action, Intention, and Biblical versus Rabbinic Weight

micro_segments.title: Yesh lahem maaseh ve-afilu mi-d'oraita: Self-evident action has biblical weight for minors
Ein lahem machshava ve-afilu mi-derabanan: Intention alone has no weight even rabbinically
Middle case (machshavto nikeret mitoch maasav): Has rabbinic weight only, no biblical weight
Philosophical synthesis: Intention (kavana) defines the type of action, not a separate criterion; for minors, only objective action analysis applies

macro_segments.title: Intentionality Requirements in Offerings (Korbanot): Contrast with Chulin

micro_segments.title: Question: How do we identify someone acting unintentionally (mitasek) when slaughtering a korban?
Torah requirement for korbanot: Shechita must be done knowing one is slaughtering an animal (shechita leshem ben bakar)
Implication for minors: Can a minor perform shechitat kedoshim? Depends on whether the action objectively reveals knowledge of the korban's status
Tosafot's expansion: Beyond knowing it is an animal, one must know it is a korban (kedoshim), not think it is chulin (unconsecrated)

macro_segments.title: Shechitat Oved Kochavim (Non-Jew's Slaughter): Categorical Exclusion and Status

micro_segments.title: Mishna: A non-Jew's slaughter is neveilah (invalid) and causes tumah even through carrying; technically correct slaughter does not validate it
The non-Jew is categorically excluded from the role of shochet—not about intention or technical skill but legal incapacity
Concern addressed: Is the non-Jew's slaughter forbidden in benefit because performed as avodah zarah (idolatry)?

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's Position: Assumption of Idolatrous Intent vs. Mainstream View

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer: Stam machshavas oved kochavim (default assumption) is for avodah zarah; slaughter is forbidden in benefit
Objection: Even if he states intent to avodah zarah, it is not an action that changes the status of your animal
Mainstream view (not Rabbi Eliezer): We do not assume stam non-Jew intends avodah zarah; animal is neveilah but not assur in benefit

macro_segments.title: Whether Minim (Apostate Jews and Committed Idolaters) Exist Among Non-Jews

micro_segments.title: Concern: Even if most non-Jews are not sincere idolaters, perhaps we should worry about the minority who are (minim)?
Response (Rav Nachman): Ein minim ba-umot ovdei kochavim—there are no minim among non-Jews; even committed ones are not considered minim
Explanation: The majority of non-Jews outside Israel (chutz la-aretz) practice idolatry as mere custom (minhag avoteihen), not sincere belief
Medieval application: Tosafot and others invoke this principle to permitting transactions with Christians, treating it as a halakhic fiction

macro_segments.title: Clarification: Minim Definition and Non-Jews Bringing Sacrifices

micro_segments.title: Different contexts for 'minim': For slaughter, a non-Jew minin is not assumed to sacrifice to idolatry; for slaughter, a Jew min is so assumed
Different contexts for execution: Both Jewish and non-Jewish minim should be killed per kal vachomer logic
Torah text (mikkem—'from you') allows non-Jews to bring sacrifices without distinction, even if idolaters; Jews (min) cannot

macro_segments.title: Tumah of Idolatrous Offerings: Comparison to Neveilah and Corpse Tumah

micro_segments.title: Shimush distinction: Non-Jew's slaughter causes tumah only by carrying (masses); idolatrous offering may cause tumah even in tent (ohel)
Rabbi Yehuda ben Beseira: Idolatrous offering is compared to corpse tumah, causing tumah in tent; debate over whether mishna follows his view

topical_tags.term: Cheresh, shoteh, katan (deaf, mentally impaired, minor)
Machshavto nikeret mitoch maasav (intention evident through action)
Kiyutan (fruit susceptible to tumah via liquid)
Mi-d'oraita vs. mi-derabanan (biblical vs. rabbinic weight)
Shechitat chulin vs. shechitat kedoshim (unconsecrated vs. consecrated slaughter)
Rabbi Eliezer position on non-Jew intent
Eretz Yisrael vs. chutz la-aretz distinction
Ohel (tent; causes tumah even without contact)
